title: Long-sentence drill
description: Complex sentences with subclauses — sustain speed through length
text: Although the meeting was scheduled for ten in the morning, several team members, including the director and the project lead, did not arrive until almost half past, which delayed the agenda by nearly forty-five minutes.

tips.text: Long sentences are the stuff of contracts, reports, and scholarship. A reader trusts you to hold the thread; so must your hands.
A clause set off by 'which' is a side road. Mind the rhythm there so you rejoin the main sentence without losing the thought.

tips.target: Although the meeting was scheduled for ten in the morning, several team members, including the director and

tips.text: Don't snatch at the comma, and don't stop for it. Let it pass under your hands at the same even speed as the rest.

tips.target: the project lead, did not arrive until almost half past, which delayed the agenda by nearly forty-five minutes.

character_tips.anna: A long sentence tests whether you can hold your focus to the end. Don't fade at the midpoint; carry it through. - Eleanor Hayes, editor
character_tips.maxim: Legal and academic writing lives at this length. Learn to sustain it and little will daunt you. - Eleanor Hayes, editor
character_tips.knopych: This one runs on. Pace yourself like a long shift, even and unhurried, and you will reach the full stop with something left. - Eleanor Hayes, editor
character_tips.klavochka: Think of it as a long phrase of music, held steady from first note to last. - Eleanor Hayes, editor
